item.title: The Bedroom
item.lesson: In October 1888, waiting for Gauguin to join him in Arles, van Gogh painted his own sparse bedroom in the Yellow House and wrote to his brother Theo that the picture was meant to express 'rest' — 'looking at the picture ought to rest the brain, or rather the imagination.' Flat color would do the work: butter-yellow bed, pale violet walls (now faded toward blue as the red pigment deteriorated), the perspective tilted like a Japanese print so the floor seems to rush toward you. He loved the composition enough to paint it three times; this version he made a year later at the asylum in Saint-Rémy, copying his own painting from memory as an act of steadying himself — the original having been water-damaged. The doubled portraits over the bed and the two chairs facing each other whisper the domestic hope of that autumn, which collapsed within weeks in the crisis that cost him part of his ear. A painting about peace, made twice by a man fighting for it.
item.lookFor: Everything comes in pairs — two pillows, two chairs, two portraits, two doors — in a room van Gogh occupied alone. Once you see it, the picture's ache is audible.
